name: Ahura Mazda
shortDescription: Ahura Mazda is the supreme creator god in Zoroastrianism, representing goodness, light, and truth. He is the eternal and uncreated source of all that is good in the world.
description: Ahura Mazda, meaning "Wise Lord," is the central figure in Zoroastrianism, the ancient Persian religion. He is the uncreated, eternal, and omniscient god who represents all that is good, true, and pure. Ahura Mazda is the creator of the universe and all that exists within it, including humanity. He is the source of light, both physical and metaphorical, and embodies wisdom, justice, and order. In Zoroastrian cosmology, Ahura Mazda is engaged in a constant struggle against Angra Mainyu (also known as Ahriman), the embodiment of evil and darkness. This cosmic battle between good and evil shapes the world and the choices of humanity. Humans are encouraged to actively participate in this struggle by choosing to follow the path of Asha, the divine order and truth, which is aligned with Ahura Mazda's will. The ultimate goal is to defeat Angra Mainyu and establish a world of perfect goodness and harmony. Ahura Mazda's attributes are often associated with fire, representing purity and divine presence. The Amesha Spentas, divine emanations or archangels, are seen as extensions of Ahura Mazda's power and assist him in maintaining the cosmic order. Worship of Ahura Mazda involves prayer, ritual, and ethical conduct, emphasizing the importance of good thoughts, good words, and good deeds. The concept of Ahura Mazda as the supreme, benevolent creator has had a profound influence on other religions and philosophical systems.

sources.title: The Religion of the Good Religion
sources.author: Frederic Barth

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Universe
keyMyths.description: Ahura Mazda created the universe in six stages: first the sky, then water, then earth, then vegetation, then animals, and finally humanity. This creation was a deliberate act of goodness intended to provide a battleground for the forces of good against the evil of Angra Mainyu.

keyMyths.title: The Cosmic Battle with Angra Mainyu
keyMyths.description: Ahura Mazda is perpetually at war with Angra Mainyu, the spirit of destruction. This cosmic struggle shapes the universe and influences the actions of humanity, who are called upon to choose between good and evil and actively participate in the battle against darkness.

keyMyths.title: The Revelation to Zoroaster
keyMyths.description: Ahura Mazda revealed himself to Zoroaster, a prophet, and tasked him with spreading the message of truth and righteousness. This revelation formed the basis of Zoroastrianism, the religion centered around the worship of Ahura Mazda and the principles of good thoughts, good words, and good deeds.

keyMyths.title: The Role of the Amesha Spentas
keyMyths.description: Ahura Mazda is assisted by the Amesha Spentas, divine beings or archangels who embody his attributes and help maintain cosmic order. These entities represent qualities like Good Thought, Righteousness, Dominion, Piety, Perfection, and Immortality and are integral to Ahura Mazda's plan for the universe.

extendedContent.title: Definition
extendedContent.content:
Ahura Mazda, literally translated as "Wise Lord" or "Lord of Wisdom," is the supreme and uncreated god in Zoroastrianism. He is the source of all goodness, truth, and light, and stands in stark opposition to Angra Mainyu (Ahriman), the embodiment of evil and darkness. Unlike many other polytheistic deities, Ahura Mazda is considered a singular, universal, and transcendent being, existing beyond the limitations of space and time. He is the ultimate creator, the architect of the cosmos, and the upholder of cosmic order, known as Asha.

The concept of Ahura Mazda is deeply intertwined with the Zoroastrian emphasis on monotheism, though some scholars interpret the religion as a form of dualism due to the existence of Angra Mainyu. However, Zoroastrians believe that Ahura Mazda is ultimately more powerful and will eventually triumph over evil, restoring the world to its perfect state. His wisdom guides humanity towards righteousness and allows them to participate in the ongoing cosmic battle between good and evil.

Understanding Ahura Mazda requires acknowledging the importance of free will in Zoroastrian thought. Humans are not mere puppets of the divine but are endowed with the capacity to choose between good and evil. By choosing to follow the path of Asha, they contribute to the strengthening of Ahura Mazda's forces and the ultimate defeat of Angra Mainyu. This active participation in the cosmic struggle is a central tenet of Zoroastrianism.

extendedContent.title: Cultural Context
extendedContent.content:
The rise of Ahura Mazda as the central deity in Zoroastrianism represents a significant shift from the polytheistic beliefs prevalent in ancient Persia before Zoroaster's teachings. Zoroaster, the prophet and founder of Zoroastrianism, preached a radical message of monotheism, elevating Ahura Mazda above all other deities and declaring him the sole object of worship. This new religious framework challenged the established social and religious order, leading to initial resistance but eventually gaining widespread acceptance, particularly among the Persian elite.

The Achaemenid emperors, such as Darius the Great and Xerxes, embraced Zoroastrianism and promoted the worship of Ahura Mazda throughout their vast empire. Inscriptions from this era frequently invoke Ahura Mazda as the protector of the king and the source of his authority. This royal patronage helped to solidify Zoroastrianism's position as the dominant religion of Persia for centuries. The concept of a divinely ordained ruler, accountable to Ahura Mazda, became a central aspect of Persian political thought.

Even after the decline of the Achaemenid Empire and the subsequent conquests by Alexander the Great and later by Islamic forces, the influence of Ahura Mazda and Zoroastrianism persisted. While Zoroastrianism eventually became a minority religion in Persia, its ethical principles, emphasis on good deeds, and belief in a final judgment continue to resonate within Persian culture and beyond. The enduring legacy of Ahura Mazda is evident in Persian art, literature, and philosophy, serving as a reminder of the profound impact of Zoroastrianism on the region's history and identity.

extendedContent.title: Related Practices
extendedContent.content:
Several practices within Zoroastrianism are directly related to the worship and veneration of Ahura Mazda. Fire, considered a symbol of Ahura Mazda's divine light and purity, plays a central role in Zoroastrian rituals. Fire temples, where a sacred flame is perpetually maintained, serve as places of worship and communion with the divine. Priests tend to the fire, reciting prayers and performing ceremonies to honor Ahura Mazda and maintain the purity of the sacred space.

Prayer is another essential practice in Zoroastrianism, with devotees offering prayers to Ahura Mazda throughout the day. These prayers often express gratitude for his blessings, seek guidance in navigating life's challenges, and reaffirm commitment to following the path of Asha. The Avesta, the sacred scripture of Zoroastrianism, contains a collection of hymns and prayers dedicated to Ahura Mazda and other divine beings.

Ethical conduct is also intrinsically linked to the worship of Ahura Mazda. Zoroastrianism emphasizes the importance of good thoughts, good words, and good deeds as the means of pleasing Ahura Mazda and contributing to the triumph of good over evil. Honesty, compassion, and generosity are highly valued virtues, and Zoroastrians strive to embody these qualities in their daily lives. By living a righteous life, they actively participate in the ongoing cosmic struggle and draw closer to Ahura Mazda's divine presence.
